posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I think we have skated around this issue for a long time. It is so overused that it in danger of becoming totally redundant. At best, it means different things to different people; whether or not the increase is due to inflation or better winemaking is one debate. Another is whether for some critics there is a benefit financial or otherwise to it.

In the end though, we will all define what we mean by a perfect wine differently. How do you?

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked: Price tag.

posts.name: Scott G r u n 3 r
posts.cooked: Means someone overrated a wine
posts.display_username: Scott G r u n 3 r

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
The perfect wine is perfect until the next perfect one. Scoring something 100 on an empirical scale simply begs for a 101 point wine in the future.

There is something to be said for a perfect wine experience (wine, people, place, time) but that cannot be scored, just described.
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I think we have skated around this issue for a long time. It is becoming for me almost redundant, as we see more and 100 point scores. The score means different things to different people; whether or not the increasing is due to inflation or better winemaking is one debate. Another is whether for some critics there is a benefit financial or otherwise to it.

In the end though, we will all define what we mean by a perfect wine differently. How do you?

Here’s what it can’t reasonably mean: It can’t be the best of all time and it can’t actually be perfect. How could anyone possibly know perfection or the apogee of wine in its totality? This seems an impossible standard, and if we take the position that no wine can be perfect, and strike the 100 point score from the metric, then 99 for all intents and purposes becomes the new relative “100-point” score leading to an identical dilemma.

So if we’re generous to critics, and tasters in that we assume that they have some dose of humility, then a 100 point score simply means that they can’t conceive of the wine being better than it is, given its terroir and perhaps even the vintage it was grown in (the vintage being a likely controversial point). Whether their assessment of quality holds up in time is anyone’s guess.

So if we’re generous to critics, and tasters in that we assume that they have some dose of humility, then a 100 point score simply means that they can’t conceive of the wine being better than it is, given its terroir and perhaps even the vintage it was grown in (the vintage being a likely controversial point). Whether their assessment of quality holds up in time is anyone’s guess.

Well written.
So when critics are handing out 99 and 100 for EP wines that isnt even bottled yet, the wines could actually end up being less than the original score. What about as the wine ages? If a barreled wine is 100, where does it go from there? What’s the purpose of aging ut if it is perfect RIGHT NOW.
This is why I think burgundy critics understand the rating system better than Bordeaux critics.

Does knowing the label before drinking the wine affect your score? If critics had to score wines blindly would they still give such high scores?

posts.name: Frederico L e e
posts.cooked: It means nothing to me except the wine is overrated and/or overpriced

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I think we have skated around this issue for a long time. It is becoming for me almost redundant, as we see more and 100 point scores. The score means different things to different people; whether or not the increasing is due to inflation or better winemaking is one debate. Another is whether for some critics there is a benefit financial or otherwise to it.

In the end though, we will all define what we mean by a perfect wine differently. How do you?

Here’s what it can’t reasonably mean: It can’t be the best of all time and it can’t actually be perfect. How could anyone possibly know perfection or the apogee of wine in its totality? This seems an impossible standard, and if we take the position that no wine can be perfect, and strike the 100 point score from the metric, then 99 for all intents and purposes becomes the new relative “100-point” score leading to an identical dilemma.

So if we’re generous to critics, and tasters in that we assume that they have some dose of humility, then a 100 point score simply means that they can’t conceive of the wine being better than it is, given its terroir and perhaps even the vintage it was grown in (the vintage being a likely controversial point). Whether their assessment of quality holds up in time is anyone’s guess.

Well written.
So when critics are handing out 99 and 100 for EP wines that isnt even bottled yet, the wines could actually end up being less than the original score. What about as the wine ages? If a barreled wine is 100, where does it go from there? What’s the purpose of aging ut if it is perfect RIGHT NOW.
This is why I think burgundy critics understand the rating system better than Bordeaux critics.

Does knowing the label before drinking the wine affect your score? If critics had to score wines blindly would they still give such high scores?

In some sense it’s always about potential - and I think this is easily assumed since wines tasted at EP are almost always tasted again in bottle and given a more final score. Taking the other position here, what use is it if Allen Meadows is putting an “exact-at-the-moment” score on La Tache at a point in time in which no reasonable person would drink it? That seems far less helpful to consumers - and despite the Vinous controversy, the essence of wine criticism is to help consumers make decisions about buying wine.

It’s an imperfect system for sure - but it is helpful to the extent consumers are willing to be reasonable and not naive about the usefulness and limits of scoring wines. For me, my concern with score inflation (much the heart of the issue with “100 point wines”) is less that the upper range is being used more often, but that the point compression ends up the same, with 88-90 point no longer rarely used for anything but $10-20 wines. In the same vein, I don’t find it particularly useful when critics treat a 94-96 point score like 98-100 point score.

posts.name: jordan jacobs
posts.cooked:
Mark,
I don’t think I know what a 100 point wine tastes like. Nor, do I know what a perfect wine would be. Have I tasted a a 100pt/perfect wine? Maybe. I have tasted many 100 point wines yet I never had the mind to score it 100 points myself. Again, I don’t know why other than how can a wine be perfect? I have tasted amazing wines where perceived flaws can be debated but I don’t think saying that a wine had no perceived flaws also made it perfect.

So to answer your question, I guess a person felt that a wine was some some combination of free from flaws and could not possibly offer more enjoyment than it is giving. However, based on that answer, we have been flooded with 100 point wines and score inflation to the point where most opinions of 100 points have become irrelevant (at least to me).

Means someone overrated a wine

Means I won’t be buying the wine in multiples down the road

Means I probably won’t be able to justify the price in 5 years

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: “There’s no place like home. There’s no place like home. There’s no place like home.”

posts.name: Bret Rooks
posts.cooked:
I agree that considering 100 points to mean “perfection” for a bottle is a non-starter, philosophically.

However, for those okay with using numbers as shorthand to communicate relative greatness of aesthetic experience, I think 100 points should be just another category like 95 or 82 or any other number in the agreed-upon scale. Each of those categories has more than one wine. Think about it as the range of “99.5 and up” if you like. I don’t use numbers much these days and haven’t bought or tried anything anyone would call a 100-point wine, but it’s never given me heartburn.

posts.cooked:
What does a 100 point score mean?

  1. Jeb D wrote the review.

  2. The Winery is paying $2,000/mo for Vinous Premium.

I’ve had a number of 100 pt CA cabs. IMO- it usually means it’s a tight, uber-ripe, over-extracted, heavily oaked, but in-balance mess.
Once they get about 10-15 yrs of age, I start understanding what the reviewer meant.

posts.name: Jonathan Reuter
posts.cooked: 100 points means the top 1% or 2% of wines of its type. Either now or in the future.

posts.cooked: It means it’s one point better than a 99 point wine.

posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
Folks love to obsess over flaws and “perfection” whatever that could possibly mean for a wine. That’s a distraction. Wines are not meant to be machined into flawless surfaces. Wines are sensory delivery vehicles. Some things that excite some are flaws to others and vice versa.

Jordan is on it. If a 100 point wine is not an experience that represents what the taster considers the pinnacle of their pleasure that can come from wine then the whole scale is meaningless. Flip it around. If a zero or 50 or whatever the bottom end is doesn’t represent the worst then what is the scale for?

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

While it might be hard, it’s certainly theoretically possible. Each score represents a bucket of quality/enjoyment/etc. It’s not like you’re only allowed to have 100 wines and then you rank them from 1 - 100. Just like with every other numerical score, 100 represents a certain bucket or range.

The perfect wine is perfect until the next perfect one. Scoring something 100 on an empirical scale simply begs for a 101 point wine in the future.

There is something to be said for a perfect wine experience (wine, people, place, time) but that cannot be scored, just described.

This. I was teasing someone who posted on a wine giving it 94 pts. I suggested that another wine which was approximately 10% better would thus deserve 104 pts. The 100 point scale would be more useful if we used all 100 points and a 100 point wine was a great rarity with plenty of wines in 50-90 range. Instead we use maybe ten of them (90-100) and really more like the 92-97 range.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked: One of the reasons I like the 20 point scale better than the 100 point scale is the absurdity of trying to quantify the difference between a 92 and a 93 point wine … it’s ridiculous, and I’d wager most/all critics would be unable to be consistent with themselves within 0 points if given the same wine in multiple tasting flights–what’s 92 now might be 91, 93 or even 94 this afternoon if tasted again. Whereas I think the 20 point scale really only has 10-11 different possible scores (15+) for “quality” wine and even fewer for “high quality” (those you’d think of as 90+ point) wines. It’s more like an academic scale … B/B+/A-/A/A+ … which brings me full circle to the question … what deserves an A+? Something that, given the circumstances, the grader can’t think of a way it could be better… if anything ever deserves an A+.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
It means absolutely nothing except for that the wine will get expensive. The plethora of young “100 point” wines given over the past 20 years is just ridiculous, honestly. A wine can’t be perfect unless it’s developed all of its subtlety, complexity, depth, and yet still remains vibrant, refreshing, and a pleasure to drink. And you can’t assign points for possibility…only what the wine is actually showing.

I’ve had some pretty amazing wines in my 30+ years of wine drinking (mostly thanks to generous friends!), and probably tasted 25,000+ wines, yet I’ve never had a wine I’d consider a perfect wine.

posts.name: Marcus Dean

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
It means absolutely nothing except for that the wine will get expensive. The plethora of young “100 point” wines given over the past 20 years is just ridiculous, honestly. A wine can’t be perfect unless it’s developed all of its subtlety, complexity, depth, and yet still remains vibrant, refreshing, and a pleasure to drink. And you can’t assign points for possibility…only what the wine is actually showing.

I’ve had some pretty amazing wines in my 30+ years of wine drinking (mostly thanks to generous friends!), and probably tasted 25,000+ wines, yet I’ve never had a wine I’d consider a perfect wine.

I don’t think that’s what it means, it just means the reviewer thinks it worthy of the top score that is within the scale they are using.

It’s like giving a high school geometry student an A+ for a semester. It doesn’t mean the student was perfect in every respect and the greatest geometry student of all time, just that he or she earned the highest score on the scale. Other kids before and after that student will get an A+ in geometry too.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Well written.
So when critics are handing out 99 and 100 for EP wines that isnt even bottled yet, the wines could actually end up being less than the original score. What about as the wine ages? If a barreled wine is 100, where does it go from there? What’s the purpose of aging ut if it is perfect RIGHT NOW.
This is why I think burgundy critics understand the rating system better than Bordeaux critics.

Does knowing the label before drinking the wine affect your score? If critics had to score wines blindly would they still give such high scores?

EP is always on potential anyway, and thats why many critics score a range for EP. As an example, LPB giving Angelus 2020 98-100 is ‘I will bet the score ends up being in this range once in bottle’, and more often than not they’re pretty consistent (of course its non blind so you can argue consistency or just reinforcing their own view, but different story).

This, for me, is why Anson’s 2019 Lafleur review really resonated:

Never the most exuberant during En Primeur, this is deep inky purple in colour with a violet rim. On the palate you get touches of iris, with an earthiness alongside, coupled with a grip of tannins that expands through the mid palate then clamps down again pretty sternly on the finish. Strong liquorice and chocolate notes, but the emphasis is clearly on slate and crushed stones, cigar box and cloves, with a strong, serious spine. I don’t give 100 during En Primeur, but this is as close as it gets, and is a reflection of just how impressively the Pomerol plateau has performed in the 2019 vintage. Stainless steel vinification then 15 months ageing in barrel, 33% new oak. Drinking Window 2027 - 2050

Note the bolding.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

I think the interesting question here that is missed is…

With the increase in quality of production in recent years, should the score range be re-set/recalibrated? I can imagine producing high quality wines consistently 50 years ago was much harder (perhaps?), whereas now it’s less hard.

Or put another way, should wine scores be bell curved?


Of course that leads to the problem that 90 points for a 1980 vintage wine might only translate to 85 points for a 2020 vintage wine, and the 100 pointers of the 1940s might only be a 95 pointer now, which I dont think would be very useful for consumers.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
The 100 point scale has a big problem, which is displayed here in this discussion: people assume that

a) that 100 points means THE ONE perfect wine, when it does not. For almost all here it seems that the think that there is just space for one wine at the top. For the critics, for myself and many others (nicely displayed on CT), there is a lot of space at the top of the pyramid. 100 points is a plateau with enough space for ALL wines which achieve that certain level of quality. Of course, not every critic, taster, drinker has the same definition of 100 pts and the same amount of space at the top. Every wine drinker has to find its own definition (and you have to find the critic which defines it similarly). I defined it once for me: Every wine that achieves good marks in these 12 (13) categories will get 100 pts:

  1.        expressive nose, palate, finish

  2.        high complexity

  3.        high aromatic precision, purity, not too ripe

  4.        evolving aroma profile

  5.        intriguing sweetness, balanced by other aroma categories

  6.        creamy texture and yet airy and light

  7.        perfectly ripe and round tannins

  8.        high but perfectly integrated acidity

  9.        very balanced and harmonious

  10.      long and expanding finish

  11.      has to create an emotional response

  12.     I have to like it, I have to like the style

(13.) extra points awarded for singularity

As an add on. The definition one has for perfection is not something steady. Taste and preferences change all the time.

b) that the 100 pts of a critic mean that the wine is perfect now, when in reality all the critics say that they try to assume which wine will reach perfection in the future/once mature. It would be not a big help for customers when a critic comes back 20 or 30 years later to tell that the wine he once scored 93 points because it really was a 93 point wine at that time is now a 100 point wine and the customer should have bought it 20 or 30 years ago.

posts.name: Alex Nichols
posts.cooked: The ratings system has become saturated with 100 point scores, and it’s overflowing with 98s and 99s. All of those seemed way more rare and elusive in the 90s and early 00s. 100 pointers seem to serve the critic these days almost more than the wine drinker/buyer. I’m not sure putting a higher standard would even be in the best interest of a critic for majority of their audience(?)

posts.name: Jim Brennan
posts.cooked:
Personally I do not agree with the concept of a 100 point wine as some sort of objective standard based solely on technical criteria. I think it’s silly, and mostly marketing nonsense sold to us by the trade and critics. It’s impossible to eliminate the experiential aspects of drinking wine. The 100 point wine is effectively a product being sold by critics to consumers in order drive influence and revenue (whereby they in turn sell that to the trade to effectively increase both of those things even further). Hence Parker’s evolution from consumer advocate into the despot of wine criticism, and now AG’s latest move at Vinous.

Consequently, I see the possibility of a 100 point wine as a once in a lifetime experience. And if it’s later eclipsed by another wine experience, then it’s either a result of time blurring that memory and/or the fact that the original wine wasn’t truly a 100 point experience. For me, it’s like Highlander… if I ever have a 100 wine experience, there can be only one.

Someone mentioned that if we don’t allow for many potential 100 point wines, then 99 becomes the new 100. I actually disagree. 100 / perfection has a psychological influence and inferred meaning that drives consumer buying patterns. While 99 point wines will certainly experience appreciation, I doubt that it would truly have the same impact.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I agree that considering 100 points to mean “perfection” for a bottle is a non-starter, philosophically.

However, for those okay with using numbers as shorthand to communicate relative greatness of aesthetic experience, I think 100 points should be just another category like 95 or 82 or any other number in the agreed-upon scale. Each of those categories has more than one wine. Think about it as the range of “99.5 and up” if you like. I don’t use numbers much these days and haven’t bought or tried anything anyone would call a 100-point wine, but it’s never given me heartburn.

Agree that it’s a bracket. A range of quality. The same way you could rate two of the wines you are blind tasting side-by-side 92, yet still rank one above the other.

As far as perfection goes, should we be going through life refusing to see any sunset as perfect? If you try to apply objective standards of measurement to art and nature, you’re just doing it wrong.

Then again, a simple wine can be joyous, perfect for what it is, perfect for the context its enjoyed in, but not be a “great” wine, and not justify a huge rating.

Ratings are a tool, a means of communication. We shouldn’t be giving too much weight to them. We can fully understand why a critic rates a wine highly - along their lines of subjective and objective standards - see the consistency of how they assess wines. But, still not enjoy those wines ourselves. We can have our own metrics. They don’t have to be bigger is better or whatever.

100 points means you find the wine truly and uncommonly great. We should be uncomfortable awarding higher ratings, especially if we don’t have the very extensive tasting experience to make such assessments, thus these discussions.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
It means that person on that day with that specific bottle of wine in that specific setting was ‘perfect’ to them - and only them. Nothing more and nothing less.

I for one do not feel it is possible to have a ‘perfect’ wine - and to me, the little ‘imperfections’ make a wine better and more appealing to me . . .

Cheers

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I think we have skated around this issue for a long time. It is so overused that it in danger of becoming totally redundant. At best, it means different things to different people; whether or not the increase is due to inflation or better winemaking is one debate. Another is whether for some critics there is a benefit financial or otherwise to it.

In the end though, we will all define what we mean by a perfect wine differently. How do you?

There is an old joke that a guy is interviewing people and asked the first interviewee, what is 2+2. The guy is a statistician and says that 2+2=4 with a 100% level of confidence. This goes on for a while and the employer finally interviews a lawyer. He asks the lawyer how much is 2+2? The lawyer goes and locks the door, pulls down the shades to the window and goes over and asks the employer, how much do you want it to be. I would say 100 points means what you want it to mean.

Statistically, since we are for the types of wines most of us drinking really dealing with a 30 point system (70-100), 100 points could be given to the best one out of every 30 or so wines you taste. So, yes, then some 100 point wines would be better than other 100 point wines. Alternatively, one could score based more on say a bell shaped curve where fewer wines are given 100 points. Still, this would not mean 100 points is a perfect wine. To leave the top wines only being for perfect wines, you probably need more of a million point scale, or more.

None of this really troubles me. Points are just points. They are what you give a wine. They have no intrinsic meaning. The world does not shift if I call something a 100 point wine and clearly some 100 point wines are better than other 100 point wines, just like some 80 point wines likely are better than other 80 point wines. I guess you could rate one wine 80+++*# and another 80±±$, but at some point doesn’t this get silly.

I know that this year you seem really obsessed with points - this has to be the third or fourth thread you have started with a very similar theme. To me, points are just a way for one person (often a wine critic) to compare different wines they have tasted and to tell you which one they like better. I don’t tend to think about wine in terms of points, but once in a while I do. And, I like critics to score in points (with tasting notes), but that is just because I remember what it was like to read wine reviews before the 100 point scale started to be used and it was really hard to finish an article and have a good idea what wine the reviewer likes better than another wine (especially with the British wine writers who were so tied to the Bordeaux trade).

I have no idea what it means for a wine to be perfect. Seems silly to me. I know people who if I had to rate them I would rate them 100 points. One would have been my father who was really a fabulous person. But was he perfect - absolutely not, he had flaws like everyone else. And, I view wine the same way. Discussing a wine as perfect seems awfully silly to me.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

Sometimes.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I don’t use points, but to me it means I caught a spectacular wine at the right time and in the right setting. (I think mood and company affect our wine experiences a fair amount.) Sometimes everything just comes together - I can still remember what my first bottle of Pousse 1990 60 Ouvrees tasted like, or the first time I had 89 Leflaive Batard.

I don’t really agree with the cynical “it’s means it’s expensive” take on this question. Besides, sometimes opening up an expensive bottle of wine you’ve been saving is part of the experience.

posts.name: Stephen Rash
posts.cooked: It means I can’t afford it.

posts.name: John Flanagan
posts.cooked:
One of the reasons I like the 20 point scale better than the 100 point scale is the absurdity of trying to quantify the difference between a 92 and a 93 point wine … it’s ridiculous, and I’d wager most/all critics would be unable to be consistent with themselves within 0 points if given the same wine in multiple tasting flights–what’s 92 now might be 91, 93 or even 94 this afternoon if tasted again. Whereas I think the 20 point scale really only has 10-11 different possible scores (15+) for “quality” wine and even fewer for “high quality” (those you’d think of as 90+ point) wines. It’s more like an academic scale … B/B+/A-/A/A+ … which brings me full circle to the question … what deserves an A+? Something that, given the circumstances, the grader can’t think of a way it could be better… if anything ever deserves an A+.

Totally agree. To me, the difference between a 1-10 scale, 1-20, 1-50, or 1-100 is merely an issue of granularity, and I don’t believe that any critic can reproducibility score the same wine (blind) over multiple times (among other wines) within 1 point. So, if the error might be +/- 3 points on any given day, then what’s the point of having such a refined scale? I joked (tongue in cheek) in an earlier post that a 100 point wine is 1 point better than a 99 point wine, meaning that 95-100 might as well mean the same thing. And if that’s the case, then the granularity is pointless.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

If they are all “100”, then they are equal, so equal ranking.

How many 1, 2 or 3 ratings have you seen on the 20 point scale?

posts.name: Bob Hoelting
posts.cooked:
“These go to 11.”
Nigel Tufnel

posts.name: LasseK
posts.cooked: It means that I can’t afford it.

posts.cooked:

So, if the error might be +/- 3 points on any given day, then what’s the point of having such a refined scale? And if that’s the case, then the granularity is pointless.

Go to any tasting of 20 wines or so (or even more wines) and try to use a 10 or even 20 point scale to rate the different wines you taste. Within about 5-10 minutes, I bet you are using pluses and minuses.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Sometimes I think we way over-think these things. While I think - using the word “think” quite a bit here - that the critics’ use of the so-called perfect rating hit a “jump the shark moment” years ago, I really do not care when a poster uses it, and will admit to when I see it, reading the note again with interest. I’ve personally rated 3 wines a 100, the 82 Mouton (twice), 89 Petrus (twice) and 89 Haut Brion (once). Every single one of those wines was flat out perfection to me, and I was fortunate in each instance to share the bottle with a close friend over a dinner, so not just a brief glimpse at a tasting (which for me, is always suspect on scoring, where a range is perhaps better).

Someone above asked the interesting question that I had not thought about: could you rank your 100-point wines. I bet every day I’d have a different ranking there, but honestly, I give the Petrus the nod. The Haut Brion is probably the most classic, text book perfect wine that I have had - the 82 HB was just right behind it. The Petrus and Mouton are just sexier, with the Petrus for the win. As I have gotten older, I have have elevated Pomerol over the left bank.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It is interesting how many different answers there are reflecting that you had better know who you are reading before making any kind of buying decisions. Jeff Leve, has 14 potential 100 point wines in his latest review (I think I got that right). That reflects the plateau approach I think, but I am sure Jeff can speak better to that than I can.

I have scored about 14 wines 100 points in my thirty years plus as a professional. I have an entirely different take stemming from the first time I ever gave a perfect score to a wine. I stopped analyzing, I stopped thinking, time stopped, and I fell in love. It was a visceral experience, and the delight came way before my intellect stepped in.

I only know 100 point wines that way. So the idea of having fourteen 100 point experiences in that short a time is completely alien to me. Everyone here who has given a perfect score will know that it is life changing, and what bucket you end up with is interesting and probably something to disclose.

posts.name: G. Keeler
posts.cooked:
I’ve probably told this story before but “100 points” always makes me think of it. I have a consultant that works for my group and he usually drops off some wine for me as a present each holiday season. A few years ago he drops by and gives me a bag with a bottle inside and I thank him for the nice gesture. He says open the bag so I do and it’s a Sloan or something like that from Napa. Not close to my style of wine but he doesn’t know that so obviously I act very thankful because it’s a very generous gift. He says “that bottle right there is 100 pts, that means it’s perfect”. I smile and say “100 pts from who?” He says “ahhhh everyone I think”.

For me, I couldn’t tell you the score of any wine in my cellar because I don’t buy wines based on scores. So when I come across 100 pt score I take it simply as that person really likes that wine.

Someone in the 2000 Bordeaux thread said something to the effect of “I don’t get Burgundy. Why would I pay $400 for a 92pt wine when I could pay $200 for a 98pt Bordeaux”. I think there are a lot of people that do that same math in their head. It makes them feel better about spending their money if a “professional” gives it a quality stamp of approval. I’m sure I fell into that when first getting into wine but after you figure out what style of wine you really connect with, the scores don’t mean anything, at least to me. Give me that $400 Burgundy any day over the $200 Bordeaux (assuming it’s a producer I enjoy) because I like Burgundy more no matter what the scores are. Same for that Napa cab I received. That is a style that I don’t connect with so the 100pts doesn’t mean anything to me. I know I have a few golf buddies that like that style of wine and so I’m saving it for them. And if they enjoy it and get excited to drink it, then thats a 100 pt wine experience for me.

posts.name: Jonathan Reuter
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

If they are all “100”, then they are equal, so equal ranking.

I disagree. If we think of quality as being a continuous measure then the probability that any two wines have the same score is precisely zero. Despite this fact, in a very large sample of wines, there may be multiple wines whose ratings round to 100. Similarly, in a very large class, it is possible for ten students to all earn an “A+”, without any of them earning exactly the same average of exams, etc.

No one reading this thread should worry, though. Even if we accept that most critics view ratings as a way to sort wines into “quality buckets” (rather than as a way to measure the holistic experience associated with sharing a great bottle with great friends over a great meal), we still get to argue about the appropriate sizes of the different buckets (should the 100 rating bucket be smaller than the 99 rating bucket), the extent to which we think critics are able to award the same rating to the same wine across multiple blind tastings, and whether ratings should reflect quality as experienced today or maximal predicted quality.

posts.name: Glenn P.
posts.cooked: If 100 is supposed to constitute “perfection”, then I would say that the “ness” of the wine is what is being evaluated. This would mean that, for example, a 100 point Burgundy means to the reviewer that this wine has all of the components and character that perfectly describes whatever he/she believes “Burgundyness” is or should be. It’s a perfect Burgundy, or Barolo, or whatever. It is completely subjective, however, for this reason and is nothing more than an opinion, albeit a professional opinion for whatever that is worth.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: I think it’s a good thing to question the very idea of a 100-point scale. For one thing, I don’t think wine arranges itself onto a single linear scale any more than food does; how many points would you give the world’s best hamburger? Is it inferior to ‘boeuf en croute’, or is that the wrong question?

posts.name: Tim F@itsch
posts.cooked: I remember going to a blind tasting once. It was a competition, held in a very fun and casual way. I remember our host, “this was a horrible wine, second lowest total score of the night, 100 points Robert Parker…” Points are bullshit.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: I understand that the late, lamented Jim Clendenen deliberately kept back inventory of a Pinot that had received a horrible score so people could taste it and see how wrong the publication was. This was in the days when scores had much stronger influence than they do now. I’ll be drinking to Jim this evening.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I think it’s a good thing to question the very idea of a 100-point scale. For one thing, I don’t think wine arranges itself onto a single linear scale any more than food does; how many points would you give the world’s best hamburger? Is it inferior to ‘boeuf en croute’, or is that the wrong question?



I understand that the late, lamented Jim Clendenen deliberately kept back inventory of a Pinot that had received a horrible score so people could taste it and see how wrong the publication was. This was in the days when scores had much stronger influence than they do now. I’ll be drinking to Jim this evening.

I shared a 68 point, and later an 82 point Ridge wine with Paul Draper. WS, of course. Both had become wonderful wines with age. Later, my intro to Bryan Harrington - we were both pouring at a charity event, and I knew Ken and Al worked for him - the first thing he did was give me a pour and ask what score WS had given it. I said about 91 or 92. WS had given it a 73, with two other publications giving it a 91 and 92 respectively. It was true to the site and comparable to other producers’ versions from the same vintage, which WS gave 90-92 pts. It made no sense.

I don’t know why people give so much weight to wine ratings, when they hardly blink at various critics rating a movie from 2 1/2 to 5 out of 5 stars. Take the ratings with 100 grains of salt. Data points that have their contexts. “I give this wine 99 grains of salt plus one data point.” Like I said, a wine can be perfect, but far from the greatest wine you’ve had. Not the same thing. I’d call the new Walter Scott rose everyone is glowing about on another thread perfect - and I agree with the 94 pt rating.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
I think it’s a good thing to question the very idea of a 100-point scale. For one thing, I don’t think wine arranges itself onto a single linear scale any more than food does; how many points would you give the world’s best hamburger? Is it inferior to ‘boeuf en croute’, or is that the wrong question?

So logarithmic then?

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
The perfect wine is perfect until the next perfect one. Scoring something 100 on an empirical scale simply begs for a 101 point wine in the future.

There is something to be said for a perfect wine experience (wine, people, place, time) but that cannot be scored, just described.

This. I was teasing someone who posted on a wine giving it 94 pts. I suggested that another wine which was approximately 10% better would thus deserve 104 pts. The 100 point scale would be more useful if we used all 100 points and a 100 point wine was a great rarity with plenty of wines in 50-90 range. Instead we use maybe ten of them (90-100) and really more like the 92-97 range.

Indeed. How long till we get a Suckling 105? (“Better than any wine I’ve ever rated 100”)

posts.cooked:
1. someone thought it is a quite good wine
  2. someone though he can make a profit with it

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked:
I appreciate this thread, because it’s a question I’ve always had.

In a similar vein, what sometimes confuses me more is to see a score of, say, 91 for similar varietals from the same region, but at drastically different price points. That always makes me wonder - are they of equal quality, so why ever buy the more expensive one?

I know it doesn’t work that way, but I’ve never really understood why.

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
I appreciate this thread, because it’s a question I’ve always had.

In a similar vein, what sometimes confuses me more is to see a score of, say, 91 for similar varietals from the same region, but at drastically different price points. That always makes me wonder - are they of equal quality, so why ever buy the more expensive one?

I know it doesn’t work that way, but I’ve never really understood why.

Simple: 1st who gave 91p?
Ratings here or from WS are quite inflatious (inflationaire?) …
91p often worth only some 85p.

2nd: you may prefer a more expensive 90p wine which is your style to a cheaper 92p wine absolutely not your style.
At least I do …

posts.name: Christine Courtney
posts.cooked: Maybe it is just that I am relatively new to wine, but I am not bothered by critic scores and 100-point wines. Instead, I find them interesting reference points, along with the tasting notes and drinking windows. Personally, I have fun scoring wine myself and comparing with what “professionals” think, which has helped calibrate my palate as far as quality, not as far as personal preference. This, coupled with temporarily losing my sense of taste and smell with COVID and going to a Union des Grand Cru Bordeaux event, in which I could taste something like 50 wines of the same Bordeaux vintage, really improved my ability to assess quality of a wine, but speaks nothing to my preference for the wine. I hope to someday come across a wine that I think is 100 points, but if in the course of my life, I taste more than one wine I think is a 100-point wine, that won’t cause me any concern either

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Maybe it is just that I am relatively new to wine, but I am not bothered by critic scores and 100-point wines. Instead, I find them interesting reference points, along with the tasting notes and drinking windows. Personally, I have fun scoring wine myself and comparing with what “professionals” think, which has helped calibrate my palate as far as quality, not as far as personal preference. This, coupled with temporarily losing my sense of taste and smell with COVID and going to a Union des Grand Cru Bordeaux event, in which I could taste something like 50 wines of the same Bordeaux vintage, really improved my ability to assess quality of a wine, but speaks nothing to my preference for the wine. I hope to someday come across a wine that I think is 100 points, but if in the course of my life, I taste more than one wine I think is a 100-point wine, that won’t cause me any concern either

You are far too rational for a discussion about critics and points. I’ve said for years that they can provide a valuable tool for consumers, especially for beginners. If for no other reason, the fact that they have the opportunity to try so many wines, that the average enthusiast would not have the time, resources, or exposure to experience. I’ve never understood why some think that it has to be an all or nothing proposition when it comes to using critics.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: I played worse than bogey golf and probably should have had more beers at the turn!

posts.name: Bruce G.
posts.cooked:
Personally, I have fun scoring wine myself and comparing with what “professionals” think, which has helped calibrate my palate as far as quality, not as far as personal preference.

Nothing wrong with that.
As long as you understand that “quality” is in many ways nothing more than an aggregate of personal preferences.

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked: Parker was once quoted that… “The difference between 95 points and 100 is pure emotion.” In other words, nothing that he can define in a review generally in terms of body, tannin, fruit, acid and balance make the difference. I think that quote shows just how much personal preference and undefinable factors can make in a wine, even for someone with his palate. There were over 500 wines reviewed by Galloni in his Napa reviews (Part 1 &2) that scored 95-100. If one looks at this through the Parker theory, they are all qualitatively identical and the rest is emotion. Emotion varies by day, time, outside factors and who knows what else. Something I try to remember. Greatness is easier experienced than defined and there is no certainty.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Personally, I have fun scoring wine myself and comparing with what “professionals” think, which has helped calibrate my palate as far as quality, not as far as personal preference.

Nothing wrong with that.
As long as you understand that “quality” is in many ways nothing more than an aggregate of personal preferences.

We sometimes see people people calibrate their ratings to critics in a way the metrics reward style, so they’ll rate big wines they don’t like higher than the medium bodied wines they do like. That’s an extreme case involving buying into some utter bullshit about objective quality, doubting their own palates and blindly guessing. Of course we can come to understand how we compare and contrast with some various critics in certain ways at least some of the time. But, a good exercise for blind tasting groups voting on the wines shows how varied perspectives are. You can align perfectly with someone on some wines and differ drastically on others. I find with one long running group a flight of 8 wines will most likely have 6 of the wines getting both first and last place votes.

What aspects of wine people enjoy vary greatly. Some people will completely overlook a super-complex light bodied red - compelling aromatics, long lingering finish. Other people treasure aromatics over all. Our biology differs so much person to person - type and quantity of various sensory receptors. We have neuroplasticity, meaning we can change our perceptions over time. We have our reference points, sense memories, which can magnify the importance of a specific aroma in a very positive or negative way.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
I played worse than bogey golf and probably should have had more beers at the turn!

Ha, I was going to say that it probably means I’m heading to the 17th tee.

I don’t use points, but to me it means I caught a spectacular wine at the right time and in the right setting. (I think mood and company affect our wine experiences a fair amount.) Sometimes everything just comes together - I can still remember what my first bottle of Pousse 1990 60 Ouvrees tasted like, or the first time I had 89 Leflaive Batard…

I think this is right. To me, scoring a wine 100 points makes sense only as a reflection on an extraordinary experience not just on the analytics. Therefore, I can’t really comprehend when someone assigns 100 points based on potential. Even though I’m generally comfortable with critics using a numerical scoring system (despite the inconsistencies and limitations), the last 1-2 points should be reserved for right-brain activity.
Regards,
Peter

posts.name: Eric Sch
posts.cooked:
How many points is a black diamond tree run with fresh soft powder in the morning haze?
How many points is racing side by side in the rain against a trusted mate in a racecar?
How many points is a giggling hug from your child?

I’m a computer nerd that loves quantification, but the best things in life aren’t measurable and don’t need points. It took me a bit to get there, but now it’s obvious. There are wines that I remember through time as a confluence of substance and situation. It’s too personal to be a metric, and my ability to express the pure details in mere words is limited.

posts.name: Bruce G.
posts.cooked:
We sometimes see people people calibrate their ratings to critics in a way the metrics reward style, so they’ll rate big wines they don’t like higher than the medium bodied wines they do like. That’s an extreme case involving buying into some utter bullshit about objective quality, doubting their own palates and blindly guessing. Of course we can come to understand how we compare and contrast with some various critics in certain ways at least some of the time. But, a good exercise for blind tasting groups voting on the wines shows how varied perspectives are. You can align perfectly with someone on some wines and differ drastically on others. I find with one long running group a flight of 8 wines will most likely have 6 of the wines getting both first and last place votes.

What aspects of wine people enjoy vary greatly. Some people will completely overlook a super-complex light bodied red - compelling aromatics, long lingering finish. Other people treasure aromatics over all. Our biology differs so much person to person - type and quantity of various sensory receptors. We have neuroplasticity, meaning we can change our perceptions over time. We have our reference points, sense memories, which can magnify the importance of a specific aroma in a very positive or negative way.

Very well said, Wes.
Many of us pre-points wine people (yes, I’m that old… not as old as Tom Hill, but still pretty old) were worried that the Parkers and the Spectators of the wine world were gonna cause some unwelcome changes via “give a man a fish” syndrome.
I think that was largely borne out, short term. But I have to admit that many of the people who have stayed with wine as a passion have by and large learned to grow beyond that which they’ve learned from the experts. That is a nice thing to see.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
How many points is a black diamond tree run with fresh soft powder in the morning haze?
How many points is racing side by side in the rain against a trusted mate in a racecar?
How many points is a giggling hug from your child?

I’m a computer nerd that loves quantification, but the best things in life aren’t measurable and don’t need points. It took me a bit to get there, but now it’s obvious. There are wines that I remember through time as a confluence of substance and situation. It’s too personal to be a metric, and my ability to express the pure details in mere words is limited.

You list some perfect things and are spot-on. We can take ourselves out of the moment with weird distractions. There’s a time to be comparing and analyzing wines and there’s a time to just enjoy a wine - the whole experience with that wine.

That recalls the difference between how a wine tastes and how a wine drinks. Too often wines are rated or valued on how they taste, but many wines that impress up front, from the first smell and the first taste grow tiring. Some more modest wines deliver more joy, a more lasting and complete experience.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
There were over 500 wines reviewed by Galloni in his Napa reviews (Part 1 &2) that scored 95-100. If one looks at this through the Parker theory, they are all qualitatively identical and the rest is emotion. Emotion varies by day, time, outside factors and who knows what else. Something I try to remember. Greatness is easier experienced than defined and there is no certainty.

This just shows the ridiculous nature of wine rating today by the critics. 500 wines in Napa Valley alone that rate over 95 points?? Scores are just used today to sell more wine and more subscriptions.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I hesitated to contribute to this thread, for obvious reasons, and many of the questions raised in this thread are quite familiar. But I wanted to give another perspective but explaining what a 100-point score I gave recently “means”; because what the score means—beyond the fact that the wine is movingly brilliant and situated among the very best of its peer group—varies wine by wine.

The example I wanted to pick is Cédric Bouchard’s 2008 Les Ursules. For me, giving that wine 100 points earlier this year, in addition to capturing my emotions on tasting the wine and how I feel it is situated in the broader context of sparkling wine, hopefully conveys a number of things. Mostly importantly, I hope it emphasizes that there are no glass ceilings limiting how Champagne is scored in The Wine Advocate: this is a Blanc de Noirs rather than a blend; from an unprestigious region that’s at the bottom of the echelle des crus; made by a grower who started a new label and not a Grande Marque with a long history. If any producers were thinking, “we’ll never get recognition for what we’re doing, given who we are and where we make wine”, I hope this review dispels that notion. The positive side of the 100-point system, in other words, is that it can disrupt hierarchies based on “received opinion” and old classifications that tend to stifle so-called “lesser” appellations or new producers. Beyond this important statement, this particular score also affirms that I think Les Usules is as good any of Cédric’s wines (is the hierarchical mentality so ever-present in the wine world that we tend to look for “grands crus” even when there are none?) even if La Bolorée and Creux d’Enfer are more caught after; and it also expresses my conviction that his wines do age—but like still wines rather than like Champagnes (this is a subject we have gotten into before so I won’t expand on the point and derail the thread).

I discussed all this with Cédric the other day when I went to taste his new releases, and he had understood all that, but also had his own point of view. He recalled how, back in the early years of his project, he was ridiculed by his neighbors and even family members: “there’s grass growing in your vineyards, you’re not taking care of them, you should apply some herbicides”; “you didn’t make the yield this year” (the yield limits in Champagne are generally taken as a target, “faire le rendement”, to attain the maximum yield, having no pejorative connotations). For him, receiving 100/100 for a wine from those early years was, as he put it, the final vindication from those close-to-home critiques. Incidentally, even Lalou Bize-Leroy said the same last month with regards to Robert Parker’s high scores for her wines back in the 1990s.

In this case, I was glad to be able to give Cédric this recognition, and indeed amazed that it remained for me to do it. And I hope others will agree that, employed this way, the highest of all scores can be meaningful and more than simply something that enflames the market.

link_counts.title: Help Me Understand A Wine Critic: James Suckling

posts.name: Paul @bbott
posts.cooked: It means nothing to me, in that I have never had, chased, tasted etc a single bottle that was rated at 100 points. The prices of such wines seem inflated compared with 98 point wines, which are themselves inflated against 96 point wines. I am not convinced that given variation between bottles, weather conditions, personal preference etc that any wine is perfect. I do compare wines. I use 5 stars with halves and + or -, so 5* for me would probably be something like 96-100 points. It simply meant though that I thought the wine was stunning. I haven’t given more than 4.5 in several years.

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked: Apparently very little these days, I have consumed a lot of good wines and only scored one as 100 points.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Apparently very little these days, I have consumed a lot of good wines and only scored one as 100 points.

Well Suckling has a few more than you!

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Another Question
If you have ten 100 point wines, can you rank them 1-10

Yes. 100.1, 100.2 … 101.

100 doesn’t mean too much, but usually worth the money you paid. I would agree it’s pricey, but not always overpriced. If you can afford it, then it’s never a thing called overpriced.

My idea is if you want to try a new label, for the 1st time, if you can afford it, try to get a high score vintage, cause that won’t be wrong. If you don’t like it, then you simply don’t like this label or winemaker. You actually save your time & money, and you could have a correct impression of this label.

If you don’t like it, then you simply don’t like this label or winemaker. You actually save your time & money, and you could have a correct impression of this label.

Depends on the wine Shafer Hillside is not worth the money in my opinion but a great Bordeaux that would be different with some age on it.

posts.name: Lorenzo F
posts.cooked: There are quite a big number of wines that go up in price regardless of scores . I might add some producers don’t even wanna get reviewed by some critics and nobody gives a sht .

posts.name: Jeff "Ozzie" Sattler
posts.cooked: It’s certainly rewarding for those hard working winemakers getting the recognition a 100 point rating brings them, but what it often does to the price…ouch!
